Title: Harriet Tubman Never Lost an Enslaved Passenger. Irena Sendler Never Gave Up a Jew. A Primary Source Record of Two Women Who Refused to Fail.
Tags: selflessness, humanity, courage, harriet-tubman, irena-sendler, chiune-sugihara, hugh-thompson, vasili-arkhipov, nicholas-winton, underground-railroad, holocaust, human-rights
Teaser: They had nothing in common. One was a woman born into American slavery who could not read. One was a Japanese diplomat trained in a bureaucracy that valued protocol above everything. One was a Polish Catholic social worker. One was a Soviet submarine officer. One was a United States Army helicopter pilot. One was a British stockbroker who kept a secret for fifty years. One was a village. What they shared was a single decision, made in a specific moment at a specific cost, to place the life directly in front of them above the system telling them to look away. ClownBinge documents two and a half centuries of that decision.

Teaser: Émile Durkheim published The Rules of Sociological Method in 1895. Michel Foucault delivered 'The Order of Discourse' in 1970. Donald Trump used the phrase 'fake news' 2,019 documented times during his first term. This article does not argue that Durkheim and Foucault predicted Trump. It argues that they described the mechanism that makes the phrase work, with precision that 130 years of political history has only confirmed. The Power of Words in Everyday Life.

Title: Herodotus Recorded 'Dark-Skinned and Woolly-Haired.' The Primary Source Record of Kemet as Black African Civilization and the Seat of Human Knowledge.
Teaser: The Greek historian Herodotus traveled to Kemet in the 5th century BCE and recorded what he saw: a dark-skinned, woolly-haired people who had already given the world mathematics, medicine, astronomy, engineering, and the solar calendar. He wrote it in the same text Western academia calls the founding document of the discipline of history. This is what the primary sources say.
